# CRISPR Gene Editing

CRISPR is a tool that lets scientists edit DNA precisely and cheaply. It is adapted
from a defence system bacteria use against viruses. A guide molecule directs a
cutting protein, commonly Cas9, to a chosen DNA sequence, where it makes a break; the
cell's repair machinery then disables a gene or inserts a new piece of DNA at that
spot.

## Promise and caution

CRISPR has accelerated research and opened paths to treat genetic diseases, improve
crops, and study how genes work. It also raises safety and ethical questions,
especially around edits that could be inherited by future generations. Off-target
cuts at unintended sites are a key technical concern, so much effort goes into making
the editing more accurate and predictable.
